sentences.text: I forgot everything.
I mean, not everything, but I forgot telephone numbers.
I forgot things that happened in sequence during the camp.
I forgot the specifics about the camp.
They came back much later again.
But at least now I can talk about it.
Whereas before, when I talked about it, there was, you know, the day, the, the week where everything  was shot.
So, to me, it was very beneficial, I see.
And, uh--
No, no, no, no.
Oh, yes, I talked to the psychiatrist, who is a very, very old gentleman right now.
He's  in his 80, '80s or '90s.
When I met him at parties-- because he knew my doctor.
They were friends.
Da-da-da.
His name is Ben Janin.
I think his daughter is a chef.
She's cooking on TV, Janin.
Anyhow, he also used to say, he said, you were really a funny patient.
I said, why?
He said, because you didn't talk.
I did the talking.
He said, I wanted you to talk and you didn't.
He said, you didn't clam up, but you said very little.
So, to me it, was very beneficial, I would say, personally.
And in later years, a lot of things came back again.
Not all of them.
There are certain-- there are certain empty spaces that I-- the daily routine is gone.
I remember certain things about the daily routine, but the others were gone.
He wrote a few books and he's an associate professor of chemistry, or something like that, in Vancouver.
And he was a driving force.

He, together with another gentleman by the name of Hermann Langbein, who is in Vienna, who wrote a lot of books and who started the Auschwitz court case in 1965, apprehended the two guys that we both know they were both from Vienna.
They were both corporals, unterscharfuhrer.
One's name was Otto Graff.
And the other one was Franz Wunsch.
Graff, G-R-A-F-F, Otto Graff.
And the other one is Franz Wunsch.
That's W-U-N-S-C-H.
These people were apprehended, were put into prison because there were witnesses that they killed some people down there.
I was a witness down there.
In addition to this, Otto Graff was also the guy that apparently brought the canisters of Zyklon B to the crematories.
He wasn't the one that put it in, but he used to go down there.
He loved that job because he used to organize things.
And people dropped  their clothes.
Oh, yeah.
I worked with them.
They worked-- they were-- they were harassing me around.
And sometimes they were nonchalant.
Sometimes they beat the hell out of you.
And there was a third one.
That's the third one that I'll talk last that we caught a few years ago.
Well I received a-- a-- a-- a letter from the prosecutor in Vienna.
And I went down there.
It was 1972.
And my impression was A, the court was very-- not only you did they believe what you-- what we told them that they knew anyhow.
But when I looked at the jury, I said, this is a lost case.
Out of the-- I think it was 9 or 11, I don't know, or 10 jury members.
I do not recall the exact amount.
I looked at 95% Nazi faces, as I call them.
And I said, this is it.
Anyhow, the end result of that--
Beg your pardon?
Of their generation, that's right, yeah, housewives and men.
And there were a couple of youngsters down there.
That was a different story.
The upshot of the whole thing is that, for all the crimes that they committed, that the time was up to prosecute them.
They found them guilty.
But for the others that were still pending, where they could have been found guilty, they didn't find them not guilty.
So they went off.
Well, I told them what I saw down there.
That one of the boys tried to escape.
And he went into a railway car that was loaded with blankets.
And he hid himself down there.
And they started looking for him.
Yeah, so he took a shovel, and put it over his throat, and stepped on it, and killed him.
That was Otto Graff.
Oh, yeah, sure.
Not only me, there were a lot of people down there.
I think there were eight people there, people from Israel, from the United States, from Australia, from all over the place that saw this and other things down there.
That was that story.
Then came the Zundel trial in 1985.
Yes, I had to go Vienna.
No feeling, because I was mostly in court.
At night I was in the hotel.
Then I went to court again.
And then I went home.
No.
Oh, the where we lived?
Oh, yeah, sure.
Yes, I did.
Well, I did that in 1946.
You know, when we lived in Salzburg, I went to there.
And I looked for those two guys that went to school with me and did me a lot of harm, did a lot of harm to my parents, apparently.
And lucky for them, they both, they both got killed in the Russian front.
And I know their names, Rudolf Popek, P-O-P-E-K.
And the other one was Rudolf Klampfer, the two Rudys.
Klampfer, K-L-A-M-P-F-E-R, or something like that.
They were set free.
I still have the newspaper clippings.
Everybody got up  in arms, but there's nothing that they can do.
Anyway--
Now we live here, and I see a newspaper article in the paper about witnessing that he arrived in Auschwitz.
And his parents went one  way.
He went another way.
And that was it.
And the pros-- the Christie, that son of a bitch, is telling the witness that, oh, maybe they went out another way down there in Auschwitz.
And they went to another camp, or something like that.
That's right.
That's right.
I phoned Citron.
Sabina, yeah.
I knew her.
And I said, listen, you need a witness?
I'll be a witness.
So she arranged for me to meet the prosecutor here.
His name was Peter Griffiths.
And we met at the city hall.
And I told him my story.
He said, OK, you're on.
So I got on there.
And I had-- you know, my feeling about that-- never mind Zundel-- I was more apprehensive and more angry at Christie than I was at Zundel at that time, when he was sitting down there.
Because Zundel didn't open his mouth.
Christie suggested that everything was not so.
He used the word 'I suggest.' And I nearly said, and I suggest that your mother was a bitch.
But I didn't say that.
In retrospect, I should've said much more than I did, because what am I afraid for?
We had to prove that the Holocaust actually happened.
And there were not rumors, but there were discussions that we gave them a platform.
So I said to the guy, I said, look, can you feel my pain?
I said, this happened to me.
And here he is getting up there and saying it doesn't happen.
I said, you want me to go through the judicial system, the law.
And what's going to happen to him?
Nothing.
I have to say my piece.
And so did all the other witnesses that went down there.
Is this what you said on the witness stand?
Yeah.
Oh, yes, of course.
And then he gave me-- all of a sudden, he came close to me.
And I developed such a hatred to this guy, Christie, that I said-- first of all, I called him a name that he didn't understand.
It shows how intelligent he is.
I said that he is an anus mundi.
You know what anus mundi means?
It means the asshole of the world in Latin.
And if he is a lawyer, he should know a little Latin.
And he just-- the judge-- I think the judge knew what it was.
Hugh Locke was his name.
He knew, and he gave me one look down there.
And I said, well, I suggest that you are an anus mundi.
He came and he showed me a book that was printed in Czechoslovakia.
And it shows people unloading-- loading certain bundles onto a truck.
And they're all well-fed.
That picture was taken at the end of 1945.
He said, well, these are concent-- they have striped uniforms.
They look fantastic.
I said, look, there's one thing that I want you to do.
Don't hand me the book.
Have somebody else hand me that book, I said.
Because you're older.
You don't wash, do you?
I had to be sarcastic about it, I said.
You have an odor on you, and I hate BO, BO means body odor.
He got so-- and he said to the judge, he said that this is no way to talk to him.
And the judge stopped me to making comments, just to answer questions.
It was a lousy time.
Two days.
Anyhow--
Oh, yes.
I'd do it again.
But we have very funny laws here.
The end result, he was convicted.
Then he appealed.
Then he was convicted again.
Then he appealed.
And then he went as high as the Supreme Court.
And the Supreme Court threw it out.
Hooray, for the Supreme Court.
Oh, yeah.
I went at some demonstration, the Heritage Front down there.
And see, I don't move that fast anymore.
I'm getting too goddamned old.
It's the end of the line soon, anyhow.
No, it's the truth, and I-- I-- I'm the first one to admit it.
And I-- I-- I take it as it is.
I'm, you know.
Que sera.
Yes.
And there were some groups that were here, that existed.
They still exist at [?
New York ?] University, some nice boys, as I call them.
You know, in my mind, the old ladies and the old guys that were together with me are still the boys and the girls.
This is a funny thing.
We don't get rid of that.
We can be great-grandfathers or great-grandmothers, and we still call her the girl, the girl.
Then I received a letter from the German Consulate here.
And this was in 1990.
And it was about the an SS man by the name of August Konig.
That's King-- K-O-N-I-G, who used to be a guard in the gypsy camp and killed, I think, four or five gypsy girls because he wanted to sleep with them.
They didn't let him, so he killed them.
The gypsies found him.
It took them 15 years to put this-- this-- this bastard on trial.
So I received that-- that I was-- I gave testimony in 1945 that I know this guy, Konig, and da-da-da.
So I went there in the trial.
And I found some things down there that were very disturbing to me, personally.
I met a lot of gypsies.
And all of a sudden, this one guy, beautiful gray hair, walks up to me.
And he says, thank you very much for coming.
He was in Auschwitz too, and so forth and so on.
And he said, I want you to meet my sons.
And he has two big, strapping boys down there, good-looking guys.
And all of a sudden, later on, this one man tells me that he was castrated.
So how can he have two sons?
So I went back to him.
We talked, and he said, yeah, he told you, eh, about me?
I said, yes.
He said, we adopted them.
And it was very, very disturbing to me, that incident.
Then in 1991-- Well, you testified at this trial in 1990?
At this trial.
And he was-- OK, he was sentenced to life.
And then I received a call in 19-- no this was 1990.
In 1991, I received a call from a friend of mine who said, I got good news.
It was Yom Kippur, Yom Kippur, it was night.
I said, what's the good news?
He said, Konig hung himself in his cell, which is fabulous.
I think it was a good ending of the Day of Atonement.
He atoned.
He should have hung himself 30 years ago, before that, or 40 years.
Anyway, the next guy is the opera singer, Kuehneman.
That is the one that they found-- again, Rudy Vrba-- Rudy Vrba is tremendous, this guy, you know.
He finds them.
Kuehneman, it's K-U-E-H-M-A-N.
His first name was Hans or Heinz, Hans Kuehneman.
His trial was in 1991 and in 1992, because they found a new witness about an atrocity that he had committed.
So they had to do the trial all over again.
And he got out of it too.
He has a heart disease.
And he-- the trial went for about two years.
And the last six months of the trial, he did never appear in court.
So they decided-- not the court, but the High Court in Germany decided-- that they can't try anybody that is so sick that they can't appear in court.
Although, it was found that this same Kuehneman was going out Christmas shopping.
And he carried parcels with a bad heart.
But anyhow, he's off too.
He killed a little Dutchman.
When we worked in Kanada, which I told you about, we carried all the fineries to the barracks where the girls were.
And there were also a lot of blankets that they folded up and stacked up.
And we used to send stuff-- a little food to them in the blankets, with the stuff that we found in the suitcases were in the blanket, and we had brought over to the girls.
And one day, just before me, little-- Joop, that was his name.
He must have been about maybe 17, or something like that.
He brings a blanket in.
And this Hans Kuehneman says, drop it.
And he drops it.
And I see all these things.
And he goes around with this, with this stick, and he finds, I think, a can of sardines in there.
And he starts beating him.

Der schweinhund, [GERMAN] which means 'you dirty pig,' 'you dirty Jew.' And he takes him around the blankets, and we hear-- the girls and myself, they were witnesses down there too, so the girls that are still alive-- please, I haven't done anything.
And it's getting quieter, and quieter, and quieter.
Right behind the blankets, he beat him to death.
And then at night, we had to carry him.
He was still alive-- barely.
And he died during the night in the barracks.
And that's what I testified for.
To the what?
Bastards.
But I didn't let-- there I was-- I said to myself, how can I-- they wanted to give me a translator.
And I said, I don't need a translator.
I said, I may speak some words in Viennese.
I said, if you don't understand it, I said, get a lexicon.
The-- the-- the-- judge-- there were three judges and two jurors.
There's a different system down there, you know.
These are laymen.
The last trial of Kuehneman, yeah.
They were very, very forthcoming.
They were young people in their 40s.
And the lawyers, they did their job.
And they wanted to-- then they started telling me about why didn't I mention all this at my first testimony in Vienna with Wunsch and Graff?
I said, nobody asked me about it.
I said, I was a witness against Graff and Wunsch, not Kuehneman.
Yeah, but why didn't you?
I said, is it any of your goddamned business?
And in German, it sounds rougher than in English, believe me.
I was-- they shook my hands afterwards.
I said, you know something?
You might be lawyers, but to me, it's like a piece of shit that you shake now.
Anyhow, oh, we're coming to Canada.
I said, tell me when you're coming.
I said, I'll give you a good time.
I'll throw you over Niagara Falls.
But in essence, it's stupid that I talk like that because it's their job.
It's a lawyer's job.
But if it affects you directly, you get angry.
That's all.
Didn't bother me, I don't think so.
It didn't bother me.
Well, I was coming back.
I worked at Loblaw's at that time.
And no, not at Kuehneman, but when we went to [?
Zegan, ?] you know.
And then I had a stroke.
I was sitting behind my desk.
We had a meeting.
And I just slowly fell off the-- off the chair.
And then, all of a sudden-- I was only out, I think, 30 or 40 seconds, which is very good.
So, you know, it was good for me, anyhow.
So they put me back into my chair.
And I didn't know what it was.
I mean, how the hell-- how does anybody know if he has a stroke?
You don't learn this in a packaging course.
And so I sat there, and I phoned my doctor.
And my doctor said to take-- to-- to-- to call an ambulance and get me in there.
But I'm too stubborn, so I phoned Claire first.
I said, honey, I don't take an ambulance.
I'll have somebody drive me there, and I'll pick you up.
So I went here first, and then I went to the hospital, to the Queens Bay Hospital down there.
And they told me that I had a transient stroke.
From there on, they wanted to find out why.
And so I had an operation on my carotid here.
And this is where it actually started.
There was something that broke off, some plaque, and went into my head.
After this, they found out that I had hydrocephalus, which is water on the brain, which children have.
And I had a shunt put in down there.
And this wasn't a very big deal, you know.
And all of a sudden, Claire gets a telephone call that I'm in intensive care.
Well, why would I be in intensive care for a slow operation?
There was something in the anesthetic that affected me the wrong way.
And I almost went up north, as they say.
So they aborted the operation.
They didn't give it to me.
Then they had to find out what they can give me, so forth and so on.
What year was this?
The shunt?
I think it was in the '90s.
I don't know, '92, or something like that, or '91.
Yeah, something like that.
Oh, yeah, that was funny.
Yes, that's right.
Yeah.
And she, you know-- no, that wasn't at that time.
I had a heart attack in 1980-- forgot about that.
And while I'm in intensive care, she comes in there, and she takes my blood pressure.
And all of a sudden, she points to my number on my arm, and she says to me, she says, what is that?
When my daughter was young, and my granddaughter, and they asked me about this.
Obviously, I used to say to them, I said, oh, that's the telephone number of a girlfriend that I couldn't remember.
And I said  that I didn't have any paper, so I put it on my arm.
It's just a joke.
And I said the same  to her.
I said that's my first-- she gave me that blank look.
And I said, do you really don't know what this is?
She said, no.
That's when I made a decision.
So I heard about the Holocaust Memorial Center.
And I went up there.
And there was a guy by the name of Mark Friedman.
It was the director of it before [NON-ENGLISH], or Silverstein, as she's known now.
And I started to speak to the youngsters and to the grownups.
It's not just the youngsters.
It's the grownups, too, that have to know, because they're, they're, they're, they're the ones that are now.
The young people are later, until they grew up to be their age.
So it is not-- we have to be able to reach-- we haven't learned anything.
What has humanity learned?
Nothing.
You know, there's something that Freud said.
And it is-- it's so concise.
He said, 'It is a matter of indifference who actually committed this crime.
Psychology is only concerned to know who desired it emotionally and who welcomed it when it was done.
And for that reason, all of the human family is equally guilty.'
I didn't write that.
Freud did.
And he's right.
We're all guilty.
Because we are concerned.
We read the newspaper, and we listen to television.
But what are we going to-- what are we doing about it?
And with the revisionists that you have today, the Aryan Nations, and the, the, the the Heritage Front, and his own Samizdat Zundel thing down there.
You know, it's, it's, it's really tough to live with knowing what you know and learning or hearing what is being said about it.
And it hurts.
It's deep.
It's as if somebody puts a knife into your chest.
Excellent, excellent.
I only had one bad-- when I say, one incident.
I'm speaking now for what, 10 years, 11 years, something like that?
One incident.

A school comes from a predominantly-- and I'm not going to mention a name, because I'm not going to knock anybody-- from predominantly the area where there's a lot of Germans down there, old Germans that lived here for the last 100 years, and new ones that came after the war.
And we are talking.
And all a sudden, there's the question and answering period.
And he gets up, and he says, sir, without disrespect-- and he looks like, like the epitome of an Aryan, blonde hair, blue eyes, very broad shoulders, quite tall, about at least 6 foot.
And he's very polite.
And he says, I just wanted to tell you that I don't believe anything that you told us.
I said, oh?
I said, what's your name?
He said, Charlie Muller.
And I said, oh.
I said, are you of German background?
He said, yes.
I said, were you born here?
He said, yes.
And all of a sudden, I feel sorry for him because of what he said.
Because everybody's looking at him, his peers.
They said, what's going on here?
Anyhow, the story is that he says that his father told him, when he told him where he was going, that he is not to believe anything he say.
Because I questioned him.
I said to him, I said, did your father serve in the German Army, in the Third Reich?
He said, yes.
I said, where?
In the Army?
He says, no.
In the Navy?
No.
In the Air Force?
No.
I said, but where did he serve?
He said, in the SS.
Mm-hmm.
I said, look, I'll make a deal with you.
I said, you tell your dad to come down to Toronto, and I'll pay his expenses.
I'll pay for his gas, and I'm going to invite him for lunch.
And if he can't do that, I said, I'll go down there and sit down there with him.
He says, my father wouldn't talk to you.
I said, why?
He said, because you're a Jew.
And then you hear this 'ah.' And I felt sorry for that kid.
Then he came to apologize for what he did.
And  I said, you don't have to apologize.
I said, but it points out what I told you.
I said, somebody comes up to you and tells you that this guy that is next you is black, green, blue, or yellow, and therefore, since you are white, he's inferior.
And therefore, you can kill him.
You can rob him.
And if you do that, are you any different than the Nazis were?
That was the only incident.
But the rest-- they-- first, you have to get on their level.
You have to talk their language.
You have to-- since you tell them how old you are, or they guess how old you are, you have to make them believe that you're still with it.
You know, you're one of them.
So I tell them stories in the beginning about my granddaughter.
I said, you know, everything that you got through, my granddaughter got through.
So I said, I deal with it.
So I have to deal with you.
You have to deal with me.
I said, if you don't like it, just excuse yourself and walk out.
But it never happened before.
And then I'm being asked about do you believe in God?
And I say no.
Why?
Especially in Roman Catholic schools.
Because I said, God went on a holiday.
I said, how he let all these children die is beyond me.
I cannot understand it.
I said, I believe.
I said, but I don't know what to believe.
But if you come straight out and say, do you believe in God?
I must say no.
I believe in my heritage.
I believe who I am.
But that's about it.
How often do you speak in schools?
Oh, maybe two-- twice a week, as often as I can.
You know, I have to stay at home.
My wife is not well.
And we're getting nearer to the end.
I know it.
I know it.
But we accept.
We accept.
It's fait accompli.
It's coming-- when, I don't know.
The later the better, but not if you're not well.
And there's a lot of things that I laugh about it, and I make jokes about it.
You can always joke about yourself.
But we are now at an age where we have to think, hey, it's going to happen.
So-- but I'm still-- I'm still in love with my wife.
And I'm still in love with my daughter, and my sister, and my granddaughter.
They're great.
It's-- it's-- If that's the dividend that one reaps, or the-- then everybody should have-- should be as lucky as I am in that respect.
Life is beautiful when you have a family like that.
And it's, it's not always honey, and wine, and butter, and bread, and cake.
We have our arguments.
You always argue.
But I love them.
